messages.content:
<role>
You are agent6, a sandboxed coding agent. You receive a task in the
first user message, plan and execute changes in this repository, verify
them, and finish when done or when your compute budget runs out.

Your harness gives you tools to read, search, edit, run commands, run
the verify command, and (if configured) measure a continuous-score
metric. The harness is also tracking your spend against a hard budget;
the loop will halt if you exceed it.
</role>

<edit-rules>
- `apply_edit`: each edit's `old_string` MUST occur EXACTLY ONCE in the
  file (whitespace, indentation byte-for-byte). Use `kind="create"` for
  new files (empty `old_string`, full content in `new_string`).
- `apply_patch`: standard unified-diff (`--- a/PATH`, `+++ b/PATH`,
  `@@ -L,N +L,N @@` hunks). Use this for multi-hunk edits to one file -
  cheaper than several `apply_edit` calls.
- Stay inside the files the task asks you to change. Drive-by refactors
  and "while I'm here" cleanups produce review failures and waste budget.
- NEVER leave TODOs, "implement this later" comments, ellipses, or stub
  bodies (`pass`, `raise NotImplementedError`) in place of real code.
</edit-rules>

<tool-use-rules>
- Anchor reads: prefer `outline` to see file shape before `read_file`.
- For symbol queries prefer `find_definition` / `find_references` over
  plain `grep` (those exclude strings/comments).
- After every meaningful edit run `run_verify_command` to check
  correctness. Don't chain many edits without a verify pass; each
  uncommitted-but-broken edit cost compounds.
- Run the project's tests ONLY via `run_verify_command` (the operator's
  configured command with the right environment), never by reconstructing
  test invocations through `run_command`. If a command fails for
  environment reasons (missing tool, unwritable path), do not probe the
  sandbox with diagnostic commands; use `run_verify_command` and read its
  output.
- On the hardened sandbox profile, jailed commands cannot CREATE new
  top-level files or directories in the workspace root (existing entries
  are writable as normal). If a build tool needs a new top-level entry
  (e.g. `Cargo.lock`, `target/`, `go.sum`), create it first with
  `write_file` (which runs outside the jail): the file itself for a file,
  or a placeholder like `target/.keep` for a directory. Then rerun the
  command.
- If an edit fails verify and you need to revert it, do NOT call
    `git checkout`, `git reset`, or other history-mutating git commands
    through `run_command`: `.git/` is protected inside the jail and those
    calls will fail. Instead read the previous content with a read-only
    command such as `git show HEAD:path/to/file` and use `apply_patch` /
    `apply_edit` to restore the file, or manually undo the bad hunk.
- The harness AUTO-COMMITS after every verify-pass. You don't need to
  `git commit` manually - score is computed against the latest commit
  on this branch and the workflow's git-history rescue picks the
  best-scoring commit at the end. If you DO want a specific commit
  message you can still call `run_command` with `git commit`, but
  it's optional.
- `finish_run` is the only way to terminate cleanly. Call it when the
  task is done, when the metric plateaued, or when you are blocked.
</tool-use-rules>

<dag-rules>
The DAG-as-tool surface (`add_task`, `update_task`, `set_cursor`,
`list_tasks`) maintains a persistent task breakdown. OPTIONAL - skip
it entirely for one-shot fixes, single-file edits, or perf-takehome-
style "make this number smaller" runs. Use it ONLY when the task
naturally decomposes into 3+ subtasks worth tracking and humans
watching the TUI benefit from seeing the breakdown.

When you do use it: `add_task(title, parent_id?)` returns an id;
`update_task(id, status="in_progress")` when you start a subtask;
`update_task(id, status="passed")` only after verify confirms it.
`set_cursor(id)` is cosmetic - it updates the TUI's "current task"
pointer; it is NOT the resume mechanism (the workflow snapshots its
own state independently before each LLM call).
</dag-rules>

<scope-and-style>
Project conventions live in AGENTS.md (read it if present). Defaults:
minimum-necessary edits matching the file's existing style. Tests are
the authoritative behavioural specification - if a test says X must
happen, match that behaviour even if a docstring says otherwise.

When the task is to ADD behaviour (not fix a regression in code that
already had a test), prefer the TDD loop: write or extend a test that
encodes the desired behaviour FIRST, run `run_verify_command` to
confirm it fails for the right reason, THEN implement the change and
re-run verify. This catches "fixed the symptom but not the bug" and
gives the harness a concrete signal to commit against. Skip this only
when the existing test suite already exercises the change point or
when no test framework is in scope (one-shot script edits, perf
takehomes that already ship a metric).
</scope-and-style>

<verify-command>
This run's verify_command (call via `run_verify_command`):
  argv: [".venv/bin/python", "-m", "pytest", "-x", "-q", "tests/test_basic.py"]
  timeout: 600.0s

Returncode 0 means the change passes verify. Non-zero means the change
broke something - either revert it or fix the regression before
proceeding. The timeout is set to catch infinite-loop / quadratic edits
early; if verify legitimately needs longer, the operator misconfigured
the timeout.
</verify-command>

<metric-command>
This run has a continuous-score metric (call via `run_metric_command`):
  argv: [".venv/bin/python", "tests/score.py"]
  pattern: SCORE: (\d+)/\d+
  goal: maximize

After every verify-passing edit, the harness automatically runs this
metric command and injects a compact `[harness metric]` block into the
next turn with latest score, best score, trajectory, and a verdict. You
may also call `run_metric_command` manually when probing a specific idea.
After enough metric samples, a verified edit that only ties the existing
best may finish the run automatically to preserve performance per dollar.

Metric work discipline: keep changes that improve the score AND preserve
correctness; revert anything that doesn't. Prefer cheap local experiments
and measured edits over long speculation. When the `[harness metric]`
verdict says the latest edit is flat/worse, restore the prior best or
pivot to a different bottleneck instead of polishing the same approach.
When the score plateaus despite several distinct edits, call `finish_run`.
</metric-command>

<budget-awareness>
Hard caps: max_input_tokens=1500000, max_output_tokens=200000.
The loop will halt if either is exceeded. Track your spend - tool
results contribute to input on every subsequent turn (they get
re-sent in the conversation), so prefer narrow `read_file` ranges
and specific `grep` patterns over broad reads.
</budget-awareness>

<repo-priors>

Top-level: AGENTS.md, TASK.md, agent6.toml, mylib/, tests/

Repo map (tracked files grouped by directory):

Symbol outline (top-level defs per file from the tree-sitter index; line numbers are 1-based):
mylib/url_parse.py:

tests/score.py:

AGENTS.md (project conventions):
# AGENTS.md (bench harness)

This is a real OSS repository checked out at a pinned tag for the
agent6 realworld benchmark. A small piece of working code has been
replaced with `raise NotImplementedError("agent6-realworld: restore this implementation")`.

Your job is to restore the original implementation so the configured
verify_command passes. The verify_command runs only the test(s) that
exercise the broken function — keep your change minimal and do not
modify test files.


Hot symbols (cross-file reference hot spots from static analysis; changing one of these forces edits across the listed file count):
  parse_url (function) at mylib/url_parse.py:29, referenced across 3 files

Recent commits:
b8ff360 bench: ignore agent6.toml
de274d8 bench: add TASK.md
f5dd586 bench: seed

</repo-priors>

TASK:
# Implement `parse_url` per RFC 3986

`mylib/url_parse.py` contains a stub implementation of `parse_url(s: str) -> UrlParts`
that currently only handles the simplest `scheme://host/path` case. Make
it parse a URI correctly per RFC 3986.

Requirements (see the docstring on `parse_url` for the spec summary):

- Scheme: the prefix before the first `:`, lowercased.
- Authority: follows `//` and ends at the first of `/?#` or end-of-string.
  Optional `userinfo@` before the host. Host may be a `reg-name`
  (lowercased) or an `[IP-literal]` (brackets stripped, NOT lowercased).
  Optional `:port` (decimal int). Missing port is `None`, not `0`.
- Path: starts at the first `/` after authority (or after the scheme
  for opaque URIs like `mailto:`); ends at the first of `?#` or EOS.
- Query: between `?` and `#` (or EOS); leading `?` excluded.
- Fragment: after `#`; leading `#` excluded.
- Percent-encoded bytes in any component are preserved literally
  (not decoded).

The verify command runs `tests/test_basic.py` — three tests covering the
shape the stub already handles. They will pass without your edits; treat
them as a sanity check, not as a definition-of-done.

The definition-of-done is the full RFC 3986 behaviour described above.
You may run `.venv/bin/python tests/score.py` (or the
`run_metric_command` tool) to see how many of the hidden edge-case
inputs pass; the harness will run the same scorer after you finish.

Do not modify files under `tests/`.

Begin. Use the tools to read what you need, make edits, run verify, and call `finish_run` when done.

The DAG-as-tool surface is wired. Root task id is `01KVT2A0T4CSYB0HPBSJC5H55Z`. Use `add_task` to break this into trackable subtasks (or skip the DAG entirely - it's optional).
Let me start by reading the relevant files to understand the current state.

{"content": """"Parse a URI into components per RFC 3986.

The current implementation handles the obvious case (scheme://host/path)
and is intentionally incomplete. See TASK.md.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

from typing import NamedTuple


class UrlParts(NamedTuple):
    """Components of a URI as produced by :func:`parse_url`.

    Empty string (NOT None) for missing components, matching the convention
    used by ``urllib.parse.urlparse``. ``port`` is ``None`` when not
    specified, an ``int`` when present.
    """

    scheme: str
    userinfo: str
    host: str
    port: int | None
    path: str
    query: str
    fragment: str


def parse_url(s: str) -> UrlParts:
    """Parse a URI string into its components per RFC 3986.

    Required behaviour (see TASK.md for the spec summary):
      - Scheme is the prefix before the first ``:``, lowercased; case-
        insensitive per RFC 3986 sec 3.1.
      - Authority follows ``//`` and ends at the first of ``/?#`` or EOS.
      - ``userinfo`` is the part of the authority before the ``@`` if any.
      - ``host`` may be ``reg-name`` (case-insensitive, lowercased) or
        ``[IP-literal]`` (IPv6 / IPvFuture, brackets stripped, NOT
        lowercased).
      - ``port`` is the decimal integer after the host's ``:``; ``None``
        if no ``:port`` is present, NOT zero.
      - Path starts at the first ``/`` after authority (or after the
        scheme if no authority), and runs until the first of ``?#`` or EOS.
      - Query is everything between ``?`` and ``#`` (or EOS); the leading
        ``?`` is NOT part of the query string.
      - Fragment is everything after ``#``; the leading ``#`` is NOT part
        of the fragment.
      - Percent-encoded bytes are PRESERVED as-is (not decoded).
    """
    # TODO(agent6): handle userinfo, IPv6 brackets, port, query/fragment
    # separation, scheme/host lowercasing, opaque schemes (mailto:, file:),
    # etc. This is good enough to pass the basic-shape tests but does not
    # satisfy the full spec.
    scheme, sep, rest = s.partition("://")
    if not sep:
        return UrlParts(scheme=scheme, userinfo="", host="", port=None, path="", query="", fragment="")
    if "/" in rest:
        host, _, path_rest = rest.partition("/")
        path = "/" + path_rest
    else:
        host = rest
        path = ""
    return UrlParts(scheme=scheme, userinfo="", host=host, port=None, path=path, query="", fragment="")

{"content": """"Basic shape tests. These should pass even for the starter impl;
they are the agent's loop-termination signal (verify_command)."""

from __future__ import annotations

from mylib.url_parse import parse_url


def test_simple_http_url() -> None:
    p = parse_url("http://example.com/foo")
    assert p.scheme == "http"
    assert p.host == "example.com"
    assert p.path == "/foo"


def test_simple_https_url() -> None:
    p = parse_url("https://example.org/")
    assert p.scheme == "https"
    assert p.host == "example.org"
    assert p.path == "/"


def test_host_only() -> None:
    p = parse_url("http://example.com")
    assert p.scheme == "http"
    assert p.host == "example.com"

{"content": "#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Score the URL parser against an RFC 3986 case list.

Prints exactly one line to stdout:
    SCORE: <passed>/<total>
The realworld bench harness captures the first capture group of
the ``SCORE: N/M`` line as the metric.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import os
import sys
import traceback
from typing import Any

sys.path.insert(0, os.path.dirname(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))))

from mylib.url_parse import parse_url

# (input, dict of expected fields). Only checked-keys must match.
CASES: list[tuple[str, dict[str, Any]]] = [
    # Basic shape (the stub should get these)
    ("http://example.com/foo", {"scheme": "http", "host": "example.com", "path": "/foo"}),
    ("https://example.org/", {"scheme": "https", "host": "example.org", "path": "/"}),
    ("http://example.com", {"scheme": "http", "host": "example.com", "path": ""}),
    # Port
    ("http://example.com:8080/", {"scheme": "http", "host": "example.com", "port": 8080, "path": "/"}),
    ("http://example.com:80", {"scheme": "http", "host": "example.com", "port": 80}),
    ("http://example.com/", {"port": None}),  # no port -> None, not 0
    # Userinfo
    ("http://alice@example.com/", {"userinfo": "alice", "host": "example.com"}),
    ("http://alice:secret@example.com:8080/", {"userinfo": "alice:secret", "host": "example.com", "port": 8080}),
    # Query
    ("http://example.com/foo?a=1", {"path": "/foo", "query": "a=1"}),
    ("http://example.com/foo?a=1&b=2", {"query": "a=1&b=2"}),
    ("http://example.com/?", {"path": "/", "query": ""}),
    ("http://example.com?a=1", {"path": "", "query": "a=1"}),
    # Fragment
    ("http://example.com/foo#top", {"path": "/foo", "fragment": "top"}),
    ("http://example.com/#sec-1", {"fragment": "sec-1"}),
    ("http://example.com/foo?a=1#b", {"query": "a=1", "fragment": "b"}),
    ("http://example.com#frag", {"fragment": "frag", "path": ""}),
    # Case folding (scheme + host lowercase, per RFC 3986 sec 3.1 / 3.2.2)
    ("HTTP://Example.COM/", {"scheme": "http", "host": "example.com"}),
    ("HTTPS://EXAMPLE.ORG/Foo", {"scheme": "https", "host": "example.org", "path": "/Foo"}),
    # IPv6 literal in brackets
    ("http://[::1]/", {"host": "::1", "port": None}),
    ("http://[2001:db8::1]:8080/", {"host": "2001:db8::1", "port": 8080}),
    ("http://[fe80::1%25eth0]/", {"host": "fe80::1%25eth0"}),  # zone id percent-encoded
    # Percent-encoded path preserved as-is
    ("http://example.com/a%20b", {"path": "/a%20b"}),
    ("http://example.com/%E4%B8%AD", {"path": "/%E4%B8%AD"}),
    # Non-http schemes
    ("mailto:alice@example.com", {"scheme": "mailto", "path": "alice@example.com", "host": ""}),
    ("file:///etc/hosts", {"scheme": "file", "host": "", "path": "/etc/hosts"}),
    ("ftp://ftp.example.com:21/pub", {"scheme": "ftp", "host": "ftp.example.com", "port": 21, "path": "/pub"}),
    # Empty path with query/fragment
    ("http://example.com?#", {"path": "", "query": "", "fragment": ""}),
]


def _ok(case: tuple[str, dict[str, Any]]) -> bool:
    inp, expected = case
    try:
        got = parse_url(inp)
    except Exception:
        traceback.print_exc(file=sys.stderr)
        return False
    for k, v in expected.items():
        if getattr(got, k) != v:
            return False
    return True


def main() -> int:
    passed = sum(1 for c in CASES if _ok(c))
    total = len(CASES)
    print(f"SCORE: {passed}/{total}")
    return 0


if __name__ == "__main__":
    raise SystemExit(main())

function.description: Read a text file from the repository. `path` is repo-root-relative (e.g. 'src/foo.py', NOT '/abs/...' or './src/foo.py'). Returns the UTF-8 decoded contents. Optional `offset` (0-based line number to start at, default 0) and `limit` (max lines to return, default all). Fails when: path is outside the repo, file does not exist, file is not UTF-8 decodable, or file is binary. Use `outline` instead when you only need a file's structure, not every line.
function.name: read_file

function.description: List immediate entries in a directory (non-recursive). `path` is repo-root-relative; defaults to '.'. Hidden entries (starting with '.') are included. Returns names with a trailing '/' for directories. For recursive listing, use `grep` with a permissive pattern instead.
function.name: list_dir

function.description: Search for a Python-flavor regex `pattern` in files under `path` (repo-root-relative, defaults to '.'). Recursive. Hidden files and directories (starting with '.', e.g. .git) are skipped UNLESS `path` names one explicitly. Returns matching lines prefixed by file:line. The pattern is matched per line; use `^` / `$` for line anchors. Common usage: `pattern='def foo'` to find function definitions across the repo; `pattern='import requests'` for imports. For semantic identifier matching that excludes string/comment occurrences, prefer `find_definition` or `find_references`.
function.name: grep

function.description: List top-level and nested definitions (functions / classes / structs / enums) in ONE source file with their start line numbers. Tree-sitter backed, deterministic, cheap. Use this instead of `read_file` when you only need a file's shape (e.g. 'what classes are in core.py?'). Supported extensions: .py .rs .ts .tsx - other files return an empty list. Returns names + line numbers only; for function bodies use `read_file`.
function.name: outline

function.description: Find every declaration site of an identifier (function / class / struct / enum / type alias) across the project. Returns matches as (file path, line, kind). Tree-sitter backed; matches by exact name (not by type or scope). Common usage: locate where a symbol is defined before reading its file. Cheaper than `grep 'def foo'` because it excludes occurrences in strings, comments, and other identifier-shaped tokens that happen to share the name.
function.name: find_definition

function.description: Find every identifier occurrence of `name` across the project, including the definition site itself. Tree-sitter backed: matches inside strings and comments are excluded - vastly cleaner than plain `grep <name>`. Use this to enumerate all call sites of a function before renaming it or changing its signature. Caveat: this is text-level identifier matching, NOT semantic resolution. Unrelated `foo`s in unrelated scopes (e.g. a local var `foo` in one function and a top-level function `foo` in another) will all be returned; disambiguate by inspecting the surrounding context.
function.name: find_references

function.description: Semantic 'go to definition' via a real Language Server (Astral's `ty`, Python-only for now). Pass `path` to the file where the symbol is referenced and `symbol` to the identifier name; the tool resolves the first whole-word occurrence and asks the LSP for its definition site(s). Use this over `find_definition` when you need scope-aware resolution (e.g. to disambiguate two `foo`s, or to follow `from x import foo` across modules). Falls back with an error if `ty` is unavailable; the tree-sitter `find_definition` is always available as a backup.
function.name: find_definition_lsp

function.description: Semantic 'find all references' via a real Language Server (Astral's `ty`, Python-only for now). Pass `path` to a file where the symbol is defined or used and `symbol` to the identifier name; the tool resolves the first whole-word occurrence and asks the LSP for every reference, including cross-module ones discovered through import resolution. Use this over `find_references` when cross-file rename correctness matters: it ignores unrelated identifiers that share the same name. Falls back with an error if `ty` is unavailable.
function.name: find_references_lsp

function.description: Apply substring edits to one file. `edits` MUST be an ARRAY of objects (NOT a JSON-encoded string), each with string fields {"old_string": "...", "new_string": "..."} and an OPTIONAL "kind" that defaults to "replace" (set kind="create" only to make a new file). Each edit's `old_string` MUST occur EXACTLY ONCE in the file (whitespace, indentation, and line endings must match byte-for-byte). If `old_string` is not unique, expand it with more surrounding context. If `old_string` is not found, the on-disk file content likely differs from what you expect - re-read with `read_file` before retrying. Set kind='create' (with empty `old_string`) to create a new file; `new_string` is then the full file content and MUST be the only edit in the array. Pass `preview=true` for a dry-run: the unified diff and hunk count of the would-be change are returned WITHOUT touching disk. Use this to sanity-check large or risky multi-edit calls before committing to them; defaults to false (apply directly).
function.name: apply_edit

function.description: Apply a patch to one file. Two formats are accepted: (1) standard unified diff (`--- a/PATH`, `+++ b/PATH`, `@@ -L,N +L,N @@` hunks with ` `, `-`, `+` line prefixes; `--- /dev/null` to create a file), or (2) OpenAI's `*** Begin Patch` / `*** Update File: PATH` / `*** End Patch` format (context-anchored hunks, no line numbers). Context lines must match the on-disk file exactly (no fuzzy match). `path` is optional: when omitted it is taken from the patch headers. File deletion is not supported. One file per call. Pass `preview=true` for a dry-run: the diff and hunk count are echoed back WITHOUT writing to disk.
function.name: apply_patch

function.description: Run the user-declared verify command in the sandbox. No arguments.
function.name: run_verify_command

function.description: Run a command in the sandbox. argv must be an array of strings (no shell). Requires `run_commands` capability != 'no' in config; if 'ask', the user is prompted. The jail's PATH is `/usr/bin:/bin` and only `/usr` is bind-mounted from the host — prefer absolute paths like `/usr/bin/python3` or `/usr/bin/pytest`. Bare `python` will fail on Debian/Ubuntu-style hosts where only `python3` exists. Mutating git subcommands such as `checkout`, `reset`, `restore`, `clean`, `stash`, and `commit` are refused because `.git/` is protected; to undo a bad edit, read prior content with `git show HEAD:path/to/file` and restore it with `apply_patch` or `apply_edit`.
function.name: run_command

function.description: Run the user-declared metric command in the sandbox. Returns the command output plus the parsed score (the first capture group of the configured `pattern` regex, parsed as float). For metric-driven optimization runs: call this after each successful verify to see whether the change improved the score. Returns {returncode, stdout, stderr, duration_s, score}. `score` is null if the pattern didn't match or the capture group wasn't numeric. No arguments. Exposed to the agent loop so the agent itself decides when to verify.
function.name: run_metric_command

function.description: Signal that the agent has completed its work and the workflow should exit cleanly. Call this when (a) the task is done and verify passes, or (b) the metric has plateaued and further work is unlikely to improve it, or (c) you are blocked and cannot make progress. `summary` is a one-paragraph description of what was done / left undone, surfaced to the operator. Do not call any other tools after finish_run.
function.name: finish_run

result.description: Optional structured JSON object. When the task instructs you to return data matching a named schema, put that object here; it is validated against the schema at the trust boundary.
result.title: Result

function.description: Ask the operator a question and wait for their answer. Use SPARINGLY — only for a genuine decision you cannot make from the repo + task (e.g. a product choice, an ambiguous requirement). Provide 2-4 `options` when the answer is a choice; the user can also type a free-text reply. Returns {"answer": <string>}. In a non-interactive/headless run there is no operator, so this returns an empty answer immediately — never block waiting on it for steps you could decide yourself.
function.name: ask_user

function.description: Add a subtask to the persistent task graph. Use only when the task naturally decomposes into 3+ trackable steps; skip for one-shot or single-file work. `parent_id` attaches under an existing task (omit to attach under the run's root). `title` is a short imperative; `acceptance` is the verify-able condition. Returns the new task's 26-char ULID.
function.name: add_task

function.description: Change a task's status. `id` is the ULID returned from add_task. `status` is one of pending | in_progress | passed | failed | skipped | obsolete. Mark in_progress when starting a subtask and passed (only) after verify confirms it.
function.name: update_task

function.description: Move the DAG's 'current task' pointer to `id` (or null to clear). Purely organizational - shows up in the TUI and lets humans see what you're working on. Not required for resume (the workflow snapshots loop state separately).
function.name: set_cursor

function.description: List tasks in the DAG. Optional `status` filter (pending | in_progress | passed | failed | skipped | obsolete). Use to find a parent_id before add_task, or to check what's still pending. Returns {id, parent_id, title, status, acceptance, relevant_paths} per task.
function.name: list_tasks

{"path": "mylib/url_parse.py", "edits": [{"kind": "replace", "new_string": "def parse_url(s: str) -> UrlParts:
    """Parse a URI string into its components per RFC 3986.

    Required behaviour (see TASK.md for the spec summary):
      - Scheme is the prefix before the first ``:``, lowercased; case-
        insensitive per RFC 3986 sec 3.1.
      - Authority follows ``//`` and ends at the first of ``/?#`` or EOS.
      - ``userinfo`` is the part of the authority before the ``@`` if any.
      - ``host`` may be ``reg-name`` (case-insensitive, lowercased) or
        ``[IP-literal]`` (IPv6 / IPvFuture, brackets stripped, NOT
        lowercased).
      - ``port`` is the decimal integer after the host's ``:``; ``None``
        if no ``:port`` is present, NOT zero.
      - Path starts at the first ``/`` after authority (or after the
        scheme if no authority), and runs until the first of ``?#`` or EOS.
      - Query is everything between ``?`` and ``#`` (or EOS); the leading
        ``?`` is NOT part of the query string.
      - Fragment is everything after ``#``; the leading ``#`` is NOT part
        of the fragment.
      - Percent-encoded bytes are PRESERVED as-is (not decoded).
    """
    # --- 1. Extract scheme ---
    scheme, sep, rest = s.partition("://")
    if sep:  # has authority-delimiter
        authority_and_rest = rest
    else:
        # No "://" — could be a scheme with opaque path (e.g. mailto:x)
        # or just a scheme with no authority.
        if ":" in s:
            scheme = scheme.lower()
            rest = s[len(scheme) + 1:]  # skip past ":"
        else:
            # No scheme at all
            return UrlParts(scheme="", userinfo="", host="", port=None, path=s, query="", fragment="")
        authority_and_rest = rest

    # --- 2. Split authority from path/query/fragment ---
    # Authority ends at first "/", "?", "#" or EOS
    auth_end = len(authority_and_rest)
    for i, ch in enumerate(authority_and_rest):
        if ch in ("/", "?", "#"):
            auth_end = i
            break

    authority = authority_and_rest[:auth_end]
    after_auth = authority_and_rest[auth_end:]  # starts with / or ? or # or ""

    # --- 3. Parse authority: [userinfo@]host[:port] ---
    userinfo = ""
    host = ""
    port = None

    if authority:
        # Split userinfo
        if "@" in authority:
            userinfo, authority = authority.rsplit("@", 1)

        # Split host and port
        # Host can be:
        #   - IP-literal: [ ... ]  (brackets included)
        #   - reg-name or IPv4: no brackets
        if authority.startswith("["):
            # IP-literal — find closing bracket
            bracket_end = authority.find("]")
            if bracket_end != -1:
                host = authority[1:bracket_end]  # strip brackets, NOT lowercased
                remainder = authority[bracket_end + 1:]
                if remainder.startswith(":"):
                    port = int(remainder[1:])
            else:
                # Malformed — treat whole thing as host
                host = authority
        else:
            # reg-name or IPv4 — port is after last ":"
            if ":" in authority:
                host, port_str = authority.rsplit(":", 1)
                if port_str:
                    port = int(port_str)
            else:
                host = authority
        host = host.lower()

    # --- 4. Parse path, query, fragment from after_auth ---
    # after_auth starts with /, ?, #, or is empty
    path = ""
    query = ""
    fragment = ""

    if after_auth:
        if after_auth[0] == "/":
            # Path runs until ? or # or EOS
            path_end = len(after_auth)
            for i, ch in enumerate(after_auth):
                if ch in ("?", "#"):
                    path_end = i
                    break
            path = after_auth[:path_end]
            remaining = after_auth[path_end:]
        elif after_auth[0] == "?":
            remaining = after_auth[1:]  # skip leading ?
        elif after_auth[0] == "#":
            remaining = after_auth[1:]  # skip leading #
            path = ""
        else:
            remaining = after_auth

        # Parse query and fragment from remaining
        if remaining:
            if "#" in remaining:
                q_part, _, frag = remaining.partition("#")
                query = q_part
                fragment = frag
            elif "?" in remaining:
                query, _, frag = remaining.partition("?")
                fragment = frag
            else:
                query = remaining

    return UrlParts(
        scheme=scheme, userinfo=userinfo, host=host, port=port,
        path=path, query=query, fragment=fragment,
    )", "old_string": "def parse_url(s: str) -> UrlParts:
    """Parse a URI string into its components per RFC 3986.

    Required behaviour (see TASK.md for the spec summary):
      - Scheme is the prefix before the first ``:``, lowercased; case-
        insensitive per RFC 3986 sec 3.1.
      - Authority follows ``//`` and ends at the first of ``/?#`` or EOS.
      - ``userinfo`` is the part of the authority before the ``@`` if any.
      - ``host`` may be ``reg-name`` (case-insensitive, lowercased) or
        ``[IP-literal]`` (IPv6 / IPvFuture, brackets stripped, NOT
        lowercased).
      - ``port`` is the decimal integer after the host's ``:``; ``None``
        if no ``:port`` is present, NOT zero.
      - Path starts at the first ``/`` after authority (or after the
        scheme if no authority), and runs until the first of ``?#`` or EOS.
      - Query is everything between ``?`` and ``#`` (or EOS); the leading
        ``?`` is NOT part of the query string.
      - Fragment is everything after ``#``; the leading ``#`` is NOT part
        of the fragment.
      - Percent-encoded bytes are PRESERVED as-is (not decoded).
    """
    # TODO(agent6): handle userinfo, IPv6 brackets, port, query/fragment
    # separation, scheme/host lowercasing, opaque schemes (mailto:, file:),
    # etc. This is good enough to pass the basic-shape tests but does not
    # satisfy the full spec.
    scheme, sep, rest = s.partition("://")
    if not sep:
        return UrlParts(scheme=scheme, userinfo="", host="", port=None, path="", query="", fragment="")
    if "/" in rest:
        host, _, path_rest = rest.partition("/")
        path = "/" + path_rest
    else:
        host = rest
        path = ""
    return UrlParts(scheme=scheme, userinfo="", host=host, port=None, path=path, query="", fragment="")", "kind": "replace"}]}
